title: The Science of Sleep: Why Your Brain Needs Rest

slides.heading: The Science of Sleep
slides.body: Why your brain desperately needs rest — and what happens when it doesn't get it

slides.heading: Sleep Is Not Passive
slides.body: Your brain is highly active during sleep. It cycles through distinct stages — light sleep, deep sleep, and REM — each serving a critical biological function.

slides.heading: The 90-Minute Cycle
slides.body: Sleep happens in roughly 90-minute cycles, typically 4–6 per night. Early cycles are dominated by deep sleep; later cycles have more REM. Missing cycles disrupts recovery.

slides.heading: Deep Sleep: Body Repair Mode
slides.body: During deep (slow-wave) sleep, the body releases growth hormone, repairs tissues, and strengthens the immune system. This is the most physically restorative stage of sleep.

slides.heading: REM: Memory & Emotion
slides.body: REM sleep is when the brain consolidates memories and processes emotions. Dreams occur here. Cutting REM short impairs learning, creativity, and emotional regulation.

slides.heading: The Glymphatic System
slides.body: While you sleep, a brain-cleaning system called the glymphatic system flushes out toxic waste — including amyloid-beta, a protein linked to Alzheimer's disease. Sleep is literally brain detox.

slides.heading: What Happens When You're Sleep-Deprived
slides.body: Even one night of poor sleep raises cortisol, impairs decision-making, and slows reaction time. Chronic sleep loss is linked to obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and depression.

slides.heading: Circadian Rhythm: Your Internal Clock
slides.body: A 24-hour biological clock regulates your sleep-wake cycle using light cues. Blue light from screens at night suppresses melatonin, tricking the brain into staying alert.

slides.heading: How Much Sleep Do You Actually Need?
slides.body: Adults need 7–9 hours per night; teenagers need 8–10. Only about 1–3% of people are true 'short sleepers' by genetics — most people who claim to thrive on 5 hours are simply adapted to feeling tired.

slides.heading: Sleep Is a Superpower
slides.body: Prioritizing sleep is one of the highest-leverage health habits you can build. Keep a consistent schedule, limit screens before bed, and protect your 7–9 hours — your brain will thank you.
